The torches are eventually piled up and burned in one location. * '''Shimenawa Ceremony''': The sacred rope (''shimenawa'') is cut, and portable shrines (''mikoshi'') descend from Yuki Shrine. Women, believed to secure safe childbirth by pulling the ropes, lead the procession. * '''Choppen Ceremony''': Men in loincloths hang onto the portable shrine’s poles in a traditional coming-of-age ritual. * '''End of the Procession''': The portable shrines are placed at the Otabisho, marking the end of the main events around midnight. * '''The Next Day''': The portable shrines are returned to Yuki Shrine during the ''kankōsai'' (return procession), concluding the festival.
== Visitor Information ==
Due to the narrow streets of Kurama, space is limited, and viewing areas can be crowded. Traffic regulations are in place, restricting vehicle access to Kurama from 3 PM to 2 AM the next day. The {{ill|Eizan Railway Kurama Line|ja|叡山電鉄鞍馬線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eizan Railway Kurama Line|qq=}} is the primary mode of transportation during the festival, but long wait times are expected due to its limited capacity. The festival is held annually on [[:en:October 22|October 22]], coinciding with the [[:en:Jidai Matsuri|Jidai Matsuri]].
The festival was registered as a Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 1, 1983.
== Event Process ==
* '''Around 6 PM''': Fires are lit in front of each household, referred to as "eji," following the call for "Gather for the sacred event!" * '''Procession of Torches''': Initially led by children carrying smaller torches (called "tokkuri"), followed by adults carrying larger torches while chanting "Saireiya, Sairyo" (meaning "Festival!"). * '''Around 8 PM''': Large torches from each neighborhood group parade through the village towards Kurama Temple’s main gate. The torches are eventually piled up and burned in one location. * '''Shimenawa Ceremony''': The sacred rope (''shimenawa'') is cut, and portable shrines (''mikoshi'') descend from Yuki Shrine. Women, believed to secure safe childbirth by pulling the ropes, lead the procession. * '''Choppen Ceremony''': Men in loincloths hang onto the portable shrine’s poles in a traditional coming-of-age ritual. * '''End of the Procession''': The portable shrines are placed at the Otabisho, marking the end of the main events around midnight. * '''The Next Day''': The portable shrines are returned to Yuki Shrine during the ''kankōsai'' (return procession), concluding the festival.
== Visitor Information ==
Due to the narrow streets of Kurama, space is limited, and viewing areas can be crowded. Traffic regulations are in place, restricting vehicle access to Kurama from 3 PM to 2 AM the next day. The {{ill|Eizan Railway Kurama Line|ja|叡山電鉄鞍馬線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eizan Railway Kurama Line|qq=}} is the primary mode of transportation during the festival, but long wait times are expected due to its limited capacity. Handling of festival-related items, including torches and mikoshi, is strictly prohibited for non-participants.

It is considered one of Kyoto's three most unusual festivals.<ref name="kyoto-np">{{Cite news|url=http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/sightseeing/article/20131022000166|title=勇壮な炎、夜空きらめく 鞍馬の火祭|newspaper =[[Kyoto Shimbun]]|date=2013-10-22|accessdate=2013-10-22}}</ref>
During the festival, torches and sacred poles ({{ill|Kenhoko|ja|剣鉾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenhoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) are carried through the village amidst the blazing light of bonfires, heading towards the shrine gate.
== Overview ==
In the 3rd year of [[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] (940), due to frequent earthquakes and unrest, [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] decided to enshrine {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Daimyōjin|ja|由岐大明神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Daimyōjin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 由岐神社}} in the northern area of Kyoto to serve as a protector. A procession accompanied by lit reeds from the [[:en:Kamogawa|Kamo River (Kyoto)]] extended for about one kilometer, which greatly moved the villagers. The Kurama Fire Festival originated from this event as a way for the local residents to commemorate the relocation and the spiritual significance of Yuki Daimyōjin. It was hosted by [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama Temple]] until the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] when [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] separated Shinto and Buddhism.
The festival is held annually on [[:en:October 22|October 22]], coinciding with the [[:en:Jidai Matsuri|Jidai Matsuri]].
The festival was registered as a Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 1, 1983.
== Event Process ==
* '''Around 6 PM''': Fires are lit in front of each household, referred to as "eji," following the call for "Gather for the sacred event!" * '''Procession of Torches''': Initially led by children carrying smaller torches (called "tokkuri"), followed by adults carrying larger torches while chanting "Saireiya, Sairyo" (meaning "Festival!"). * '''Around 8 PM''': Large torches from each neighborhood group parade through the village towards Kurama Temple’s main gate. The torches are eventually piled up and burned in one location. * '''Shimenawa Ceremony''': The sacred rope (''shimenawa'') is cut, and portable shrines (''mikoshi'') descend from Yuki Shrine. Women, believed to secure safe childbirth by pulling the ropes, lead the procession. * '''Choppen Ceremony''': Men in loincloths hang onto the portable shrine’s poles in a traditional coming-of-age ritual. * '''End of the Procession''': The portable shrines are placed at the Otabisho, marking the end of the main events around midnight. * '''The Next Day''': The portable shrines are returned to Yuki Shrine during the ''kankōsai'' (return procession), concluding the festival.
== Visitor Information ==
Due to the narrow streets of Kurama, space is limited, and viewing areas can be crowded. Traffic regulations are in place, restricting vehicle access to Kurama from 3 PM to 2 AM the next day. The {{ill|Eizan Railway Kurama Line|ja|叡山電鉄鞍馬線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eizan Railway Kurama Line|qq=}} is the primary mode of transportation during the festival, but long wait times are expected due to its limited capacity. Handling of festival-related items, including torches and mikoshi, is strictly prohibited for non-participants.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11663900]] Wikidata name: Kurama Himatsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox_holiday | Event Name = Kurama Fire Festival (鞍馬の火祭) | image = File:Fire festival of KURAMA Kyoto, Japan.jpg | image Description = Parade through the village | Type = [[:en:Festival|Festival]] | Event Period = [[:en:October|October]] | Venue = [[:en:Kurama|Kurama, Kyoto]], [[:en:Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] }} The '''Kurama Fire Festival''' (鞍馬の火祭, ''Kurama no Himatsuri'') is one of the annual festivals of {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|由岐神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}} located in [[:en:Kurama|Kurama, Kyoto]]. It is considered one of Kyoto's three most unusual festivals.<ref name="kyoto-np">{{Cite news|url=http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/sightseeing/article/20131022000166|title=勇壮な炎、夜空きらめく 鞍馬の火祭|newspaper =[[Kyoto Shimbun]]|date=2013-10-22|accessdate=2013-10-22}}</ref>
During the festival, torches and sacred poles ({{ill|Kenhoko|ja|剣鉾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenhoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) are carried through the village amidst the blazing light of bonfires, heading towards the shrine gate.
== Overview ==
In the 3rd year of [[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] (940), due to frequent earthquakes and unrest, [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] decided to enshrine {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Daimyōjin|ja|由岐大明神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Daimyōjin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 由岐神社}} in the northern area of Kyoto to serve as a protector. A procession accompanied by lit reeds from the [[:en:Kamogawa|Kamo River (Kyoto)]] extended for about one kilometer, which greatly moved the villagers. The Kurama Fire Festival originated from this event as a way for the local residents to commemorate the relocation and the spiritual significance of Yuki Daimyōjin. It was hosted by [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama Temple]] until the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] when [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] separated Shinto and Buddhism.
The festival is held annually on [[:en:October 22|October 22]], coinciding with the [[:en:Jidai Matsuri|Jidai Matsuri]].
The festival was registered as a Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 1, 1983.
== Event Process ==
* '''Around 6 PM''': Fires are lit in front of each household, referred to as "eji," following the call for "Gather for the sacred event!" * '''Procession of Torches''': Initially led by children carrying smaller torches (called "tokkuri"), followed by adults carrying larger torches while chanting "Saireiya, Sairyo" (meaning "Festival!"). * '''Around 8 PM''': Large torches from each neighborhood group parade through the village towards Kurama Temple’s main gate. The torches are eventually piled up and burned in one location. * '''Shimenawa Ceremony''': The sacred rope (''shimenawa'') is cut, and portable shrines (''mikoshi'') descend from Yuki Shrine. Women, believed to secure safe childbirth by pulling the ropes, lead the procession. * '''Choppen Ceremony''': Men in loincloths hang onto the portable shrine’s poles in a traditional coming-of-age ritual. * '''End of the Procession''': The portable shrines are placed at the Otabisho, marking the end of the main events around midnight. * '''The Next Day''': The portable shrines are returned to Yuki Shrine during the ''kankōsai'' (return procession), concluding the festival.
== Visitor Information ==
Due to the narrow streets of Kurama, space is limited, and viewing areas can be crowded. Traffic regulations are in place, restricting vehicle access to Kurama from 3 PM to 2 AM the next day. The {{ill|Eizan Railway Kurama Line|ja|叡山電鉄鞍馬線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eizan Railway Kurama Line|qq=|1=Kurama Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410729}} is the primary mode of transportation during the festival, but long wait times are expected due to its limited capacity. Handling of festival-related items, including torches and mikoshi, is strictly prohibited for non-participants.
== Images ==
<gallery> File:鞍馬の火祭1.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭2.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭3.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭4.jpg| </gallery>

== Related Articles ==

In the 3rd year of [[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] (940), due to frequent earthquakes and unrest, [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] decided to enshrine {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Daimyōjin|ja|由岐大明神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Daimyōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 由岐神社}} in the northern area of Kyoto to serve as a protector. A procession accompanied by lit reeds from the [[:en:Kamogawa|Kamo River (Kyoto)]] extended for about one kilometer, which greatly moved the villagers. The Kurama Fire Festival originated from this event as a way for the local residents to commemorate the relocation and the spiritual significance of Yuki Daimyōjin. It was hosted by [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama Temple]] until the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] when [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] separated Shinto and Buddhism.
The festival is held annually on [[:en:October 22|October 22]], coinciding with the [[:en:Jidai Matsuri|Jidai Matsuri]].
The festival was registered as a Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 1, 1983.
== Event Process ==
* '''Around 6 PM''': Fires are lit in front of each household, referred to as "eji," following the call for "Gather for the sacred event!" * '''Procession of Torches''': Initially led by children carrying smaller torches (called "tokkuri"), followed by adults carrying larger torches while chanting "Saireiya, Sairyo" (meaning "Festival!"). * '''Around 8 PM''': Large torches from each neighborhood group parade through the village towards Kurama Temple’s main gate. The torches are eventually piled up and burned in one location. * '''Shimenawa Ceremony''': The sacred rope (''shimenawa'') is cut, and portable shrines (''mikoshi'') descend from Yuki Shrine. Women, believed to secure safe childbirth by pulling the ropes, lead the procession. * '''Choppen Ceremony''': Men in loincloths hang onto the portable shrine’s poles in a traditional coming-of-age ritual. * '''End of the Procession''': The portable shrines are placed at the Otabisho, marking the end of the main events around midnight. * '''The Next Day''': The portable shrines are returned to Yuki Shrine during the ''kankōsai'' (return procession), concluding the festival.
== Visitor Information ==
Due to the narrow streets of Kurama, space is limited, and viewing areas can be crowded. Traffic regulations are in place, restricting vehicle access to Kurama from 3 PM to 2 AM the next day. The {{ill|Eizan Railway Kurama Line|ja|叡山電鉄鞍馬線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eizan Railway Kurama Line|1=Kurama Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410729}} is the primary mode of transportation during the festival, but long wait times are expected due to its limited capacity. <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Kurama Fire Festival}} {{wikidata link|Q11663900}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kurama Fire Festival]][[en:Kurama Fire Festival]][[ja:鞍馬の火祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11663900]] Wikidata name: Kurama Himatsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox_holiday | Event Name = Kurama Fire Festival (鞍馬の火祭) | image = File:Fire festival of KURAMA Kyoto, Japan.jpg | image Description = Parade through the village | Type = [[:en:Festival|Festival]] | Event Period = [[:en:October|October]] | Venue = [[:en:Kurama|Kurama, Kyoto]], [[:en:Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] }} The '''Kurama Fire Festival''' (鞍馬の火祭, ''Kurama no Himatsuri'') is one of the annual festivals of {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|由岐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}} located in [[:en:Kurama|Kurama, Kyoto]]. It is considered one of Kyoto's three most unusual festivals.<ref name="kyoto-np">{{Cite news|url=http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/sightseeing/article/20131022000166|title=勇壮な炎、夜空きらめく 鞍馬の火祭|newspaper =[[Kyoto Shimbun]]|date=2013-10-22|accessdate=2013-10-22}}</ref>
During the festival, torches and sacred poles ({{ill|Kenhoko|ja|剣鉾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenhoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) are carried through the village amidst the blazing light of bonfires, heading towards the shrine gate.
== Overview ==
In the 3rd year of [[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] (940), due to frequent earthquakes and unrest, [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] decided to enshrine {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Daimyōjin|ja|由岐大明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Daimyōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 由岐神社}} in the northern area of Kyoto to serve as a protector. A procession accompanied by lit reeds from the [[:en:Kamogawa|Kamo River (Kyoto)]] extended for about one kilometer, which greatly moved the villagers. The Kurama Fire Festival originated from this event as a way for the local residents to commemorate the relocation and the spiritual significance of Yuki Daimyōjin. It was hosted by [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama Temple]] until the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] when [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] separated Shinto and Buddhism.
The festival is held annually on [[:en:October 22|October 22]], coinciding with the [[:en:Jidai Matsuri|Jidai Matsuri]].
The festival was registered as a Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 1, 1983.
== Event Process ==
* '''Around 6 PM''': Fires are lit in front of each household, referred to as "eji," following the call for "Gather for the sacred event!" * '''Procession of Torches''': Initially led by children carrying smaller torches (called "tokkuri"), followed by adults carrying larger torches while chanting "Saireiya, Sairyo" (meaning "Festival!"). * '''Around 8 PM''': Large torches from each neighborhood group parade through the village towards Kurama Temple’s main gate. The torches are eventually piled up and burned in one location. * '''Shimenawa Ceremony''': The sacred rope (''shimenawa'') is cut, and portable shrines (''mikoshi'') descend from Yuki Shrine. Women, believed to secure safe childbirth by pulling the ropes, lead the procession. * '''Choppen Ceremony''': Men in loincloths hang onto the portable shrine’s poles in a traditional coming-of-age ritual. * '''End of the Procession''': The portable shrines are placed at the Otabisho, marking the end of the main events around midnight. * '''The Next Day''': The portable shrines are returned to Yuki Shrine during the ''kankōsai'' (return procession), concluding the festival.
== Visitor Information ==
Due to the narrow streets of Kurama, space is limited, and viewing areas can be crowded. Traffic regulations are in place, restricting vehicle access to Kurama from 3 PM to 2 AM the next day. The {{ill|Eizan Railway Kurama Line|ja|叡山電鉄鞍馬線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eizan Railway Kurama Line|1=Kurama Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410729}} is the primary mode of transportation during the festival, but long wait times are expected due to its limited capacity. Handling of festival-related items, including torches and mikoshi, is strictly prohibited for non-participants.

<gallery> File:鞍馬の火祭1.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭2.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭3.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭4.jpg| </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kurama Fire Festival]][[en:Kurama Fire Festival]][[ja:鞍馬の火祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11663900]] Wikidata name: Kurama Himatsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox_holiday | Event Name = Kurama Fire Festival (鞍馬の火祭) | image = File:Fire festival of KURAMA Kyoto, Japan.jpg | image Description = Parade through the village | Type = [[:en:Festival|Festival]] | Event Period = [[:en:October|October]] | Venue = [[:en:Kurama|Kurama, Kyoto]], [[:en:Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] }} The '''Kurama Fire Festival''' (鞍馬の火祭, ''Kurama no Himatsuri'') is one of the annual festivals of {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|由岐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}} located in [[:en:Kurama|Kurama, Kyoto]]. It is considered one of Kyoto's three most unusual festivals.<ref name="kyoto-np">{{Cite news|url=http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/sightseeing/article/20131022000166|title=勇壮な炎、夜空きらめく 鞍馬の火祭|newspaper =[[Kyoto Shimbun]]|date=2013-10-22|accessdate=2013-10-22}}</ref>
During the festival, torches and sacred poles ({{ill|Kenhoko|ja|剣鉾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenhoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) are carried through the village amidst the blazing light of bonfires, heading towards the shrine gate.
== Overview ==
In the 3rd year of [[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] (940), due to frequent earthquakes and unrest, [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] decided to enshrine {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Daimyōjin|ja|由岐大明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Daimyōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 由岐神社}} in the northern area of Kyoto to serve as a protector. A procession accompanied by lit reeds from the [[:en:Kamogawa|Kamo River (Kyoto)]] extended for about one kilometer, which greatly moved the villagers. The Kurama Fire Festival originated from this event as a way for the local residents to commemorate the relocation and the spiritual significance of Yuki Daimyōjin. It was hosted by [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama Temple]] until the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] when [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] separated Shinto and Buddhism.
The festival is held annually on [[:en:October 22|October 22]], coinciding with the [[:en:Jidai Matsuri|Jidai Matsuri]].
The festival was registered as a Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 1, 1983.
== Event Process ==
* '''Around 6 PM''': Fires are lit in front of each household, referred to as "eji," following the call for "Gather for the sacred event!" * '''Procession of Torches''': Initially led by children carrying smaller torches (called "tokkuri"), followed by adults carrying larger torches while chanting "Saireiya, Sairyo" (meaning "Festival!"). * '''Around 8 PM''': Large torches from each neighborhood group parade through the village towards Kurama Temple’s main gate. The torches are eventually piled up and burned in one location. * '''Shimenawa Ceremony''': The sacred rope (''shimenawa'') is cut, and portable shrines (''mikoshi'') descend from Yuki Shrine. Women, believed to secure safe childbirth by pulling the ropes, lead the procession. * '''Choppen Ceremony''': Men in loincloths hang onto the portable shrine’s poles in a traditional coming-of-age ritual. * '''End of the Procession''': The portable shrines are placed at the Otabisho, marking the end of the main events around midnight. * '''The Next Day''': The portable shrines are returned to Yuki Shrine during the ''kankōsai'' (return procession), concluding the festival.
== Visitor Information ==
Due to the narrow streets of Kurama, space is limited, and viewing areas can be crowded. Traffic regulations are in place, restricting vehicle access to Kurama from 3 PM to 2 AM the next day. The {{ill|Eizan Railway Kurama Line|ja|叡山電鉄鞍馬線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eizan Railway Kurama Line|1=Kurama Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410729}} is the primary mode of transportation during the festival, but long wait times are expected due to its limited capacity. Handling of festival-related items, including torches and mikoshi, is strictly prohibited for non-participants.
== Images ==
<gallery> File:鞍馬の火祭1.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭2.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭3.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭4.jpg| </gallery>

== Related Articles ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:কুরামা ফায়ার ফেস্টিভ্যাল]][[commons:Category:Kurama Fire Festival]][[en:Kurama Fire Festival]][[ja:鞍馬の火祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:কুরামা ফায়ার ফেস্টিভ্যাল]][[commons:Category:Kurama Fire Festival]][[en:Kurama Fire Festival]][[ja:鞍馬の火祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kurama Fire Festival]][[en:Kurama Fire Festival]][[ja:鞍馬の火祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11663900]] Wikidata name: Kurama Himatsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox_holiday | Event Name = Kurama Fire Festival (鞍馬の火祭) | image = File:Fire festival of KURAMA Kyoto, Japan.jpg | image Description = Parade through the village | Type = [[:en:Festival|Festival]] | Event Period = [[:en:October|October]] | Venue = [[:en:Kurama|Kurama, Kyoto]], [[:en:Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] }} The '''Kurama Fire Festival''' (鞍馬の火祭, ''Kurama no Himatsuri'') is one of the annual festivals of {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|由岐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}} located in [[:en:Kurama|Kurama, Kyoto]]. It is considered one of Kyoto's three most unusual festivals.<ref name="kyoto-np">{{Cite news|url=http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/sightseeing/article/20131022000166|title=勇壮な炎、夜空きらめく 鞍馬の火祭|newspaper =[[Kyoto Shimbun]]|date=2013-10-22|accessdate=2013-10-22}}</ref>
During the festival, torches and sacred poles ({{ill|Kenhoko|ja|剣鉾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenhoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) are carried through the village amidst the blazing light of bonfires, heading towards the shrine gate.
== Overview ==
In the 3rd year of [[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] (940), due to frequent earthquakes and unrest, [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] decided to enshrine {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Daimyōjin|ja|由岐大明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Daimyōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 由岐神社}} in the northern area of Kyoto to serve as a protector. A procession accompanied by lit reeds from the [[:en:Kamogawa|Kamo River (Kyoto)]] extended for about one kilometer, which greatly moved the villagers. The Kurama Fire Festival originated from this event as a way for the local residents to commemorate the relocation and the spiritual significance of Yuki Daimyōjin. It was hosted by [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama Temple]] until the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] when [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] separated Shinto and Buddhism.
The festival is held annually on [[:en:October 22|October 22]], coinciding with the [[:en:Jidai Matsuri|Jidai Matsuri]].
The festival was registered as a Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 1, 1983.
== Event Process ==
* '''Around 6 PM''': Fires are lit in front of each household, referred to as "eji," following the call for "Gather for the sacred event!" * '''Procession of Torches''': Initially led by children carrying smaller torches (called "tokkuri"), followed by adults carrying larger torches while chanting "Saireiya, Sairyo" (meaning "Festival!"). * '''Around 8 PM''': Large torches from each neighborhood group parade through the village towards Kurama Temple’s main gate. The torches are eventually piled up and burned in one location. * '''Shimenawa Ceremony''': The sacred rope (''shimenawa'') is cut, and portable shrines (''mikoshi'') descend from Yuki Shrine. Women, believed to secure safe childbirth by pulling the ropes, lead the procession. * '''Choppen Ceremony''': Men in loincloths hang onto the portable shrine’s poles in a traditional coming-of-age ritual. * '''End of the Procession''': The portable shrines are placed at the Otabisho, marking the end of the main events around midnight. * '''The Next Day''': The portable shrines are returned to Yuki Shrine during the ''kankōsai'' (return procession), concluding the festival.
== Visitor Information ==
Due to the narrow streets of Kurama, space is limited, and viewing areas can be crowded. Traffic regulations are in place, restricting vehicle access to Kurama from 3 PM to 2 AM the next day. The {{ill|Eizan Railway Kurama Line|ja|叡山電鉄鞍馬線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eizan Railway Kurama Line|1=Kurama Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410729}} is the primary mode of transportation during the festival, but long wait times are expected due to its limited capacity. Handling of festival-related items, including torches and mikoshi, is strictly prohibited for non-participants.
== Images ==
<gallery> File:鞍馬の火祭1.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭2.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭3.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭4.jpg| </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:কুরামা ফায়ার ফেস্টিভ্যাল]][[commons:Category:Kurama Fire Festival]][[en:Kurama Fire Festival]][[ja:鞍馬の火祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:কুরামা ফায়ার ফেস্টিভ্যাল]][[commons:Category:Kurama Fire Festival]][[en:Kurama Fire Festival]][[ja:鞍馬の火祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kurama Fire Festival]][[en:Kurama Fire Festival]][[ja:鞍馬の火祭]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11663900]] Wikidata name: Kurama Himatsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox_holiday | Event Name = Kurama Fire Festival (鞍馬の火祭) | image = File:Fire festival of KURAMA Kyoto, Japan.jpg | image Description = Parade through the village | Type = [[:en:Festival|Festival]] | Event Period = [[:en:October|October]] | Venue = [[:en:Kurama|Kurama, Kyoto]], [[:en:Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] }} The '''Kurama Fire Festival''' (鞍馬の火祭, ''Kurama no Himatsuri'') is one of the annual festivals of {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|由岐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}} located in [[:en:Kurama|Kurama, Kyoto]]. It is considered one of Kyoto's three most unusual festivals.<ref name="kyoto-np">{{Cite news|url=http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/sightseeing/article/20131022000166|title=勇壮な炎、夜空きらめく 鞍馬の火祭|newspaper =[[Kyoto Shimbun]]|date=2013-10-22|accessdate=2013-10-22}}</ref>
During the festival, torches and sacred poles ({{ill|Kenhoko|ja|剣鉾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenhoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) are carried through the village amidst the blazing light of bonfires, heading towards the shrine gate.
== Overview ==
In the 3rd year of [[:en:Tengyō|Tengyō]] (940), due to frequent earthquakes and unrest, [[:en:Emperor Suzaku|Emperor Suzaku]] decided to enshrine {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Daimyōjin|ja|由岐大明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Daimyōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 由岐神社}} in the northern area of Kyoto to serve as a protector. A procession accompanied by lit reeds from the [[:en:Kamogawa|Kamo River (Kyoto)]] extended for about one kilometer, which greatly moved the villagers. The Kurama Fire Festival originated from this event as a way for the local residents to commemorate the relocation and the spiritual significance of Yuki Daimyōjin. It was hosted by [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama Temple]] until the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] when [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] separated Shinto and Buddhism.
The festival is held annually on [[:en:October 22|October 22]], coinciding with the [[:en:Jidai Matsuri|Jidai Matsuri]].
The festival was registered as a Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 1, 1983.
== Event Process ==
* '''Around 6 PM''': Fires are lit in front of each household, referred to as "eji," following the call for "Gather for the sacred event!" * '''Procession of Torches''': Initially led by children carrying smaller torches (called "tokkuri"), followed by adults carrying larger torches while chanting "Saireiya, Sairyo" (meaning "Festival!"). * '''Around 8 PM''': Large torches from each neighborhood group parade through the village towards Kurama Temple’s main gate. The torches are eventually piled up and burned in one location. * '''Shimenawa Ceremony''': The sacred rope (''shimenawa'') is cut, and portable shrines (''mikoshi'') descend from Yuki Shrine. Women, believed to secure safe childbirth by pulling the ropes, lead the procession. * '''Choppen Ceremony''': Men in loincloths hang onto the portable shrine’s poles in a traditional coming-of-age ritual. * '''End of the Procession''': The portable shrines are placed at the Otabisho, marking the end of the main events around midnight. * '''The Next Day''': The portable shrines are returned to Yuki Shrine during the ''kankōsai'' (return procession), concluding the festival.
== Visitor Information ==
Due to the narrow streets of Kurama, space is limited, and viewing areas can be crowded. Traffic regulations are in place, restricting vehicle access to Kurama from 3 PM to 2 AM the next day. The {{ill|Eizan Railway Kurama Line|ja|叡山電鉄鞍馬線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eizan Railway Kurama Line|1=Kurama Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410729}} is the primary mode of transportation during the festival, but long wait times are expected due to its limited capacity. Handling of festival-related items, including torches and mikoshi, is strictly prohibited for non-participants.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:কুরামা ফায়ার ফেস্টিভ্যাল]][[co:commons:Category:Kurama Fire Festival]][[en:Kurama Fire Festival]][[ja:鞍馬の火祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bn:কুরামা ফায়ার ফেস্টিভ্যাল]][[co:commons:Category:Kurama Fire Festival]][[en:Kurama Fire Festival]][[ja:鞍馬の火祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kurama Fire Festival]][[en:Kurama Fire Festival]][[ja:鞍馬の火祭]]
{{Infobox_holiday | Event Name = Kurama Fire Festival (鞍馬の火祭) | image = File:Fire festival of KURAMA Kyoto, Japan.jpg | image Description = Parade through the village | Type = {{ill|Festival|en|Festival|WD=Q132241|lt=Festival}} | Event Period = {{ill|October|en|October|WD=Q124|lt=October}} | Venue = {{ill|Kurama|en|Kurama|WD=Q4247779|lt=Kurama, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}} }} The '''Kurama Fire Festival''' (鞍馬の火祭, ''Kurama no Himatsuri'') is one of the annual festivals of {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|由岐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}} located in {{ill|Kurama|en|Kurama|WD=Q4247779|lt=Kurama, Kyoto}}. It is considered one of Kyoto's three most unusual festivals.<ref name="kyoto-np">{{Cite news|url=http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/sightseeing/article/20131022000166|title=勇壮な炎、夜空きらめく 鞍馬の火祭|newspaper =[[Kyoto Shimbun]]|date=2013-10-22|accessdate=2013-10-22}}</ref>
During the festival, torches and sacred poles ({{ill|Kenhoko|ja|剣鉾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenhoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) are carried through the village amidst the blazing light of bonfires, heading towards the shrine gate.
== Overview ==
In the 3rd year of {{ill|Tengyō|en|Tengyō|WD=Q1327340|lt=Tengyō}} (940), due to frequent earthquakes and unrest, {{ill|Emperor Suzaku|en|Emperor Suzaku|WD=Q361019|lt=Emperor Suzaku}} decided to enshrine {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Daimyōjin|ja|由岐大明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Daimyōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 由岐神社}} in the northern area of Kyoto to serve as a protector. A procession accompanied by lit reeds from the {{ill|Kamogawa|en|Kamogawa|WD=Q31476|lt=Kamo River (Kyoto)}} extended for about one kilometer, which greatly moved the villagers. The Kurama Fire Festival originated from this event as a way for the local residents to commemorate the relocation and the spiritual significance of Yuki Daimyōjin. It was hosted by {{ill|Kurama-dera|en|Kurama-dera|WD=Q608668|lt=Kurama Temple}} until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}} when {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} separated Shinto and Buddhism.
The festival is held annually on {{ill|October 22|en|October 22|WD=Q2957|lt=October 22}}, coinciding with the {{ill|Jidai Matsuri|en|Jidai Matsuri|WD=Q912124|lt=Jidai Matsuri}}.
The festival was registered as a Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 1, 1983.
== Event Process ==
* '''Around 6 PM''': Fires are lit in front of each household, referred to as "eji," following the call for "Gather for the sacred event!" * '''Procession of Torches''': Initially led by children carrying smaller torches (called "tokkuri"), followed by adults carrying larger torches while chanting "Saireiya, Sairyo" (meaning "Festival!"). * '''Around 8 PM''': Large torches from each neighborhood group parade through the village towards Kurama Temple’s main gate. The torches are eventually piled up and burned in one location. * '''Shimenawa Ceremony''': The sacred rope (''shimenawa'') is cut, and portable shrines (''mikoshi'') descend from Yuki Shrine. Women, believed to secure safe childbirth by pulling the ropes, lead the procession. * '''Choppen Ceremony''': Men in loincloths hang onto the portable shrine’s poles in a traditional coming-of-age ritual. * '''End of the Procession''': The portable shrines are placed at the Otabisho, marking the end of the main events around midnight. * '''The Next Day''': The portable shrines are returned to Yuki Shrine during the ''kankōsai'' (return procession), concluding the festival.
== Visitor Information ==
Due to the narrow streets of Kurama, space is limited, and viewing areas can be crowded. Traffic regulations are in place, restricting vehicle access to Kurama from 3 PM to 2 AM the next day. The {{ill|Eizan Railway Kurama Line|ja|叡山電鉄鞍馬線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eizan Railway Kurama Line|1=Kurama Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410729}} is the primary mode of transportation during the festival, but long wait times are expected due to its limited capacity. Handling of festival-related items, including torches and mikoshi, is strictly prohibited for non-participants.
== Images ==
<gallery> File:鞍馬の火祭1.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭2.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭3.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭4.jpg| </gallery>

* '''Around 6 PM''': Fires are lit in front of each household, referred to as "eji," following the call for "Gather for the sacred event!" * '''Procession of Torches''': Initially led by children carrying smaller torches (called "tokkuri"), followed by adults carrying larger torches while chanting "Saireiya, Sairyo" (meaning "Festival!"). * '''Around 8 PM''': Large torches from each neighborhood group parade through the village towards Kurama Temple’s main gate. The torches are eventually piled up and burned in one location. * '''Shimenawa Ceremony''': The sacred rope (''shimenawa'') is cut, and portable shrines (''mikoshi'') descend from Yuki Shrine. Women, believed to secure safe childbirth by pulling the ropes, lead the procession. * '''Choppen Ceremony''': Men in loincloths hang onto the portable shrine’s poles in a traditional coming-of-age ritual. * '''End of the Procession''': The portable shrines are placed at the Otabisho, marking the end of the main events around midnight. * '''The Next Day''': The portable shrines are returned to Yuki Shrine during the ''kankōsai'' (return procession), concluding the festival.
== Visitor Information ==
Due to the narrow streets of Kurama, space is limited, and viewing areas can be crowded. Traffic regulations are in place, restricting vehicle access to Kurama from 3 PM to 2 AM the next day. The {{ill|Eizan Railway Kurama Line|ja|叡山電鉄鞍馬線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eizan Railway Kurama Line|1=Kurama Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410729}} is the primary mode of transportation during the festival, but long wait times are expected due to its limited capacity. Handling of festival-related items, including torches and mikoshi, is strictly prohibited for non-participants.

{{Infobox_holiday | Event Name = Kurama Fire Festival (鞍馬の火祭) | image = File:Fire festival of KURAMA Kyoto, Japan.jpg | image Description = Parade through the village | Type = {{ill|Festival|en|Festival|WD=Q132241|lt=Festival|1=Festival}} | Event Period = {{ill|October|en|October|WD=Q124|lt=October|1=October}} | Venue = {{ill|Kurama|en|Kurama|WD=Q4247779|lt=Kurama, Kyoto|1=Kurama}}, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} }} The '''Kurama Fire Festival''' (鞍馬の火祭, ''Kurama no Himatsuri'') is one of the annual festivals of {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|由岐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}} located in {{ill|Kurama|en|Kurama|WD=Q4247779|lt=Kurama, Kyoto|1=Kurama}}. It is considered one of Kyoto's three most unusual festivals.<ref name="kyoto-np">{{Cite news|url=http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/sightseeing/article/20131022000166|title=勇壮な炎、夜空きらめく 鞍馬の火祭|newspaper =[[Kyoto Shimbun]]|date=2013-10-22|accessdate=2013-10-22}}</ref>
During the festival, torches and sacred poles ({{ill|Kenhoko|ja|剣鉾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenhoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) are carried through the village amidst the blazing light of bonfires, heading towards the shrine gate.
== Overview ==
In the 3rd year of {{ill|Tengyō|en|Tengyō|WD=Q1327340|lt=Tengyō|1=Tengyō}} (940), due to frequent earthquakes and unrest, {{ill|Emperor Suzaku|en|Emperor Suzaku|WD=Q361019|lt=Emperor Suzaku|1=Emperor Suzaku}} decided to enshrine {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Daimyōjin|ja|由岐大明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Daimyōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 由岐神社}} in the northern area of Kyoto to serve as a protector. A procession accompanied by lit reeds from the {{ill|Kamogawa|en|Kamogawa|WD=Q31476|lt=Kamo River (Kyoto)|1=Kamogawa}} extended for about one kilometer, which greatly moved the villagers. The Kurama Fire Festival originated from this event as a way for the local residents to commemorate the relocation and the spiritual significance of Yuki Daimyōjin. It was hosted by {{ill|Kurama-dera|en|Kurama-dera|WD=Q608668|lt=Kurama Temple|1=Kurama-dera}} until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}} when {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} separated Shinto and Buddhism.
The festival is held annually on {{ill|October 22|en|October 22|WD=Q2957|lt=October 22|1=October 22}}, coinciding with the {{ill|Jidai Matsuri|en|Jidai Matsuri|WD=Q912124|lt=Jidai Matsuri|1=Jidai Matsuri}}.
The festival was registered as a Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 1, 1983.

The torches are eventually piled up and burned in one location. * '''Shimenawa Ceremony''': The sacred rope (''shimenawa'') is cut, and portable shrines (''mikoshi'') descend from Yuki Shrine. Women, believed to secure safe childbirth by pulling the ropes, lead the procession. * '''Choppen Ceremony''': Men in loincloths hang onto the portable shrine’s poles in a traditional coming-of-age ritual. * '''End of the Procession''': The portable shrines are placed at the Otabisho, marking the end of the main events around midnight. * '''The Next Day''': The portable shrines are returned to Yuki Shrine during the ''kankōsai'' (return procession), concluding the festival.

The {{ill|Eizan Railway Kurama Line|ja|叡山電鉄鞍馬線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eizan Railway Kurama Line|1=Kurama Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410729}} is the primary mode of transportation during the festival, but long wait times are expected due to its limited capacity. {{Infobox_holiday | Event Name = Kurama Fire Festival (鞍馬の火祭) | image = File:Fire festival of KURAMA Kyoto, Japan.jpg | image Description = Parade through the village | Type = {{ill|Festival|en|Festival|WD=Q132241|lt=Festival|1=Festival}} | Event Period = {{ill|October|en|October|WD=Q124|lt=October|1=October}} | Venue = {{ill|Kurama|en|Kurama|WD=Q4247779|lt=Kurama, Kyoto|1=Kurama}}, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} }} The '''Kurama Fire Festival''' (鞍馬の火祭, ''Kurama no Himatsuri'') is one of the annual festivals of {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|由岐神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|qq=}} located in {{ill|Kurama|en|Kurama|WD=Q4247779|lt=Kurama, Kyoto|1=Kurama}}. It is considered one of Kyoto's three most unusual festivals.<ref name="kyoto-np">{{Cite news|url=http://www.kyoto-np.co.jp/sightseeing/article/20131022000166|title=勇壮な炎、夜空きらめく 鞍馬の火祭|newspaper =[[Kyoto Shimbun]]|date=2013-10-22|accessdate=2013-10-22}}</ref>
During the festival, torches and sacred poles ({{ill|Kenhoko|ja|剣鉾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenhoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) are carried through the village amidst the blazing light of bonfires, heading towards the shrine gate.
== Overview ==
In the 3rd year of {{ill|Tengyō|en|Tengyō|WD=Q1327340|lt=Tengyō|1=Tengyō}} (940), due to frequent earthquakes and unrest, {{ill|Emperor Suzaku|en|Emperor Suzaku|WD=Q361019|lt=Emperor Suzaku|1=Emperor Suzaku}} decided to enshrine {{ill|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)|lt=Yuki Daimyōjin|ja|由岐大明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuki Daimyōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 由岐神社}} in the northern area of Kyoto to serve as a protector. A procession accompanied by lit reeds from the {{ill|Kamogawa|en|Kamogawa|WD=Q31476|lt=Kamo River (Kyoto)|1=Kamogawa}} extended for about one kilometer, which greatly moved the villagers. The Kurama Fire Festival originated from this event as a way for the local residents to commemorate the relocation and the spiritual significance of Yuki Daimyōjin. It was hosted by {{ill|Kurama-dera|en|Kurama-dera|WD=Q608668|lt=Kurama Temple|1=Kurama-dera}} until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}} when {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} separated Shinto and Buddhism.
The festival is held annually on {{ill|October 22|en|October 22|WD=Q2957|lt=October 22|1=October 22}}, coinciding with the {{ill|Jidai Matsuri|en|Jidai Matsuri|WD=Q912124|lt=Jidai Matsuri|1=Jidai Matsuri}}.
The festival was registered as a Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Property on June 1, 1983.
== Event Process ==
* '''Around 6 PM''': Fires are lit in front of each household, referred to as "eji," following the call for "Gather for the sacred event!" * '''Procession of Torches''': Initially led by children carrying smaller torches (called "tokkuri"), followed by adults carrying larger torches while chanting "Saireiya, Sairyo" (meaning "Festival!"). * '''Around 8 PM''': Large torches from each neighborhood group parade through the village towards Kurama Temple’s main gate. The torches are eventually piled up and burned in one location. * '''Shimenawa Ceremony''': The sacred rope (''shimenawa'') is cut, and portable shrines (''mikoshi'') descend from Yuki Shrine. Women, believed to secure safe childbirth by pulling the ropes, lead the procession. * '''Choppen Ceremony''': Men in loincloths hang onto the portable shrine’s poles in a traditional coming-of-age ritual. * '''End of the Procession''': The portable shrines are placed at the Otabisho, marking the end of the main events around midnight. * '''The Next Day''': The portable shrines are returned to Yuki Shrine during the ''kankōsai'' (return procession), concluding the festival.
== Visitor Information ==
Due to the narrow streets of Kurama, space is limited, and viewing areas can be crowded. Traffic regulations are in place, restricting vehicle access to Kurama from 3 PM to 2 AM the next day. The {{ill|Eizan Railway Kurama Line|ja|叡山電鉄鞍馬線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eizan Railway Kurama Line|1=Kurama Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410729}} is the primary mode of transportation during the festival, but long wait times are expected due to its limited capacity. Handling of festival-related items, including torches and mikoshi, is strictly prohibited for non-participants.
== Images ==
<gallery> File:鞍馬の火祭1.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭2.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭3.jpg| File:鞍馬の火祭4.jpg| </gallery>

== Event Process ==
* '''Around 6 PM''': Fires are lit in front of each household, referred to as "eji," following the call for "Gather for the sacred event!" * '''Procession of Torches''': Initially led by children carrying smaller torches (called "tokkuri"), followed by adults carrying larger torches while chanting "Saireiya, Sairyo" (meaning "Festival!"). * '''Around 8 PM''': Large torches from each neighborhood group parade through the village towards Kurama Temple’s main gate. The torches are eventually piled up and burned in one location. * '''Shimenawa Ceremony''': The sacred rope (''shimenawa'') is cut, and portable shrines (''mikoshi'') descend from Yuki Shrine. Women, believed to secure safe childbirth by pulling the ropes, lead the procession. * '''Choppen Ceremony''': Men in loincloths hang onto the portable shrine’s poles in a traditional coming-of-age ritual. * '''End of the Procession''': The portable shrines are placed at the Otabisho, marking the end of the main events around midnight. * '''The Next Day''': The portable shrines are returned to Yuki Shrine during the ''kankōsai'' (return procession), concluding the festival.
== Visitor Information ==
Due to the narrow streets of Kurama, space is limited, and viewing areas can be crowded. Traffic regulations are in place, restricting vehicle access to Kurama from 3 PM to 2 AM the next day. The {{ill|Eizan Railway Kurama Line|ja|叡山電鉄鞍馬線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eizan Railway Kurama Line|1=Kurama Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11410729}} is the primary mode of transportation during the festival, but long wait times are expected due to its limited capacity. Handling of festival-related items, including torches and mikoshi, is strictly prohibited for non-participants.
